1:
"This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him;" The witnesses note the heading of Adam's generations and man made in God's likeness. On "This is the book of the generations of Adam," the grammatical voices note the heading. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "This is the book of the generations (toledoth) of Adam" — the formula introduces the genealogy of Adam through Seth's line to Noah (ch. 5); "the book" suggests a written record or register of the generations; the godly line of promise is traced.

On "In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the divine image. Calvin and Gill note that man was made "in the likeness of God" (1:26-27) — recalling the dignity of man's creation in God's image; the genealogy opens by reminding the reader that man, despite the fall and the death that runs through the chapter, was made in God's likeness; the original dignity is set against the mortality that follows.

On "in the likeness of God made he him," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the contrast: man was made in God's likeness, yet (v.3) Adam begat a son in his own (fallen) likeness; the chapter holds together the divine image in which man was made and the death which sin brought; the image of God marks man's worth, the refrain "and he died" marks the wages of sin. The verse displays the heading of Adam's generations and man made in God's likeness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the heading — "This is the book of the generations of Adam," the toledoth-formula introducing the genealogy to Noah (Keil, Ellicott); "in the likeness of God made he him," man's creation in God's image recalled (1:26-27, Calvin, Gill); the divine image set against the mortality of the chapter (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the generations-heading. The verse marks the book: of generations. The verse displays the man: made in God's likeness. The verse displays the dignity: the image. The verse displays the line: Adam's. The heading displays the godly line traced from man made in God's image. The verse displays the generations: introduced. The verse displays the likeness: of God. The verse displays the man: created. The heading of Adam's generations and man made in God's likeness display the opening of the genealogy: this is the book of the generations of Adam, made in the likeness of God — the line of promise traced, with man's original dignity in God's image recalled at the head of the chapter. The verse displays the made man, blessed and named (v.2).
2:
"Male and female created he them; and blessed them, and called their name Adam, in the day when they were created." The brief verse notes mankind created male and female, blessed, and named Adam. The grammatical voices note the two sexes. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Male and female created he them" (1:27) — God created mankind as male and female; both sexes are God's creation; the recapitulation of the creation of man recalls the original constitution of the race in the two sexes.

On "blessed them," the reformed Calvin notes the blessing. Calvin and Gill note that God "blessed them" (1:28) — the blessing of fruitfulness and dominion given to mankind at creation; God's favor rested on the race he made; the blessing recalls God's original purpose for man.

On "called their name Adam," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the naming of mankind: God "called their name Adam (man)" — the name "Adam" denotes both the first man and mankind (the race); the man and the woman together are called "Adam," for the woman is of the man and one with him; the name marks the unity of the race in its head; (the same name, Adam, for the individual and for mankind). The verse displays mankind created male and female, blessed, and named Adam.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the two sexes — "Male and female created he them," mankind created male and female (1:27, Keil, Gill); "blessed them," God's blessing of fruitfulness and dominion (1:28, Calvin, Gill); "called their name Adam," the name for the first man and the race, marking the unity of mankind (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named mankind. The verse marks the sexes: male and female. The verse displays the blessing: God's. The verse displays the name: Adam. The verse displays the race: one in Adam. The recapitulation displays mankind created, blessed, and named in Adam. The verse displays the male and female: created. The verse displays the blessing: given. The verse displays the name: Adam. Mankind created male and female, blessed, and named Adam, displays the recapitulation of man's creation: God created them male and female, blessed them, and called their name Adam — the race created in the two sexes, blessed by God, and united in the name of its head, Adam. The verse displays the named race, in whose line Adam begets Seth (v.3).
3:
"And Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in his own likeness, after his image; and called his name Seth:" The witnesses note Adam begetting Seth in his own image — the transmission of the fallen likeness. On "Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son," the grammatical voices note the genealogy's start. Ellicott and Gill note that "Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son" — the genealogy begins with Adam begetting Seth at 130 years (after the loss of Abel and the line of Cain); the godly line continues through Seth (4:25); the chapter traces this line to Noah.

On "in his own likeness, after his image," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed Calvin note the fallen image. Clarke and Calvin note that Adam begat Seth "in his own likeness, after his image" — in pointed contrast to Adam himself, made in the likeness of God (v.1; 1:26); Adam, now fallen, begat a son in his own (now corrupt, mortal) likeness; the natural likeness of father to son includes the transmission of the fallen, sinful nature (original sin propagated); Seth, like all Adam's offspring, inherits the Adamic, fallen image, not the unfallen divine image in its integrity.

On "called his name Seth," the grammatical-historical note the named son: "called his name Seth (appointed)" — Seth, the appointed seed (4:25), in whose line the promise continues; the godly line is traced from Adam through Seth; the head of the line of promise. The verse displays Adam begetting Seth in his own image.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the begetting — "Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son," the godly line continuing through Seth (4:25, Ellicott, Gill); "in his own likeness, after his image," Seth begotten in Adam's fallen likeness, the transmission of the corrupt nature, in contrast to the divine image (v.1; 1:26, Clarke, Calvin); "called his name Seth," the appointed head of the line of promise (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the begotten Seth. The verse marks the age: 130 years. The verse displays the son: Seth. The verse displays the likeness: Adam's own (fallen). The verse displays the contrast: with the divine image. The begetting displays Seth born in the fallen Adamic image. The verse displays the Adam: begetting. The verse displays the image: his own. The verse displays the Seth: named. Adam begetting Seth in his own image displays the transmission of the fallen likeness: Adam begat Seth in his own likeness, after his image — Seth born, not in the unfallen divine image, but in Adam's now-fallen likeness, the corrupt nature transmitted to his offspring, yet the godly line of promise continuing through Seth. The verse displays the begotten Seth, in Adam's long life and death (vv.4-5).
4:
"And the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years: and he begat sons and daughters:" The brief verse notes Adam's long life and many children after Seth. The grammatical voices note the years. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years" — Adam lived eight hundred years after Seth's birth; the great longevity of the antediluvians; Adam's long life allowed him to see many generations of his descendants; the patriarch lived through the early history of the race.

On "eight hundred years," the witnesses note the longevity. Gill and Calvin note that the long lifespans of the antediluvians (here, Adam's eight hundred years after Seth) are taken as literal years; the great age of the patriarchs before the flood served to people the earth rapidly and to preserve and transmit the knowledge of God and the promise through few generations; God's purpose in the long lives of the early fathers.

On "he begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the multiplied offspring: "he begat sons and daughters" — Adam had many other children besides Seth (and Cain and Abel); the race multiplied through his numerous offspring; the genealogy names only the line of promise (Seth), but notes the many sons and daughters; the rapid increase of mankind. The verse displays Adam's long life and many children after Seth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the years — "the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years," Adam's great longevity, eight hundred years after Seth (Keil, Gill); the long lifespans taken as literal, serving God's purpose to people the earth and preserve the promise (Gill, Calvin); "he begat sons and daughters," Adam's many other children, the race multiplying (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the long-lived Adam. The verse marks the years: eight hundred. The verse displays the longevity: great. The verse displays the children: sons and daughters. The verse displays the increase: of the race. The long life displays Adam living and multiplying after Seth. The verse displays the years: many. The verse displays the offspring: numerous. The verse displays the race: increasing. Adam's long life and many children after Seth display the longevity of the antediluvians: Adam lived eight hundred years after Seth and begat sons and daughters — the great age of the patriarch and the rapid multiplying of the race, the genealogy noting the line of promise yet the many other offspring. The verse displays the multiplying race, before Adam's death (v.5).
5:
"And all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years: and he died." The witnesses note the total of Adam's years and his death — the first toll of the refrain. On "all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years," the grammatical voices note the sum. Ellicott and Gill note that "all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years" — the full span of Adam's life, nine hundred and thirty years; the great age of the first man; Adam lived nearly a thousand years, witnessing the early generations of the race he fathered.

On "nine hundred and thirty years," the reformed Calvin notes the long life and its end. Calvin and Gill note that Adam's long life, though great, came to an end; the longest life is finite; even Adam, who lived nigh a thousand years, died at last; the length of life could not exempt him from the sentence of death.

On "and he died," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the refrain. Henry notes that "and he died" — the solemn refrain that tolls through the chapter (vv.8, 11, 14, etc.); the sentence of death (2:17; 3:19, "dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return") takes effect; despite the long lives, every patriarch dies; "and he died" is the seal of the curse on sin, repeated over each name; death reigns from Adam (Romans 5:14); the long lives end in the grave, save Enoch (v.24). The verse displays the total of Adam's years and his death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years," the full span of Adam's great life (Ellicott, Gill); the long life finite, ending in death (Calvin, Gill); "and he died," the refrain of the chapter, the sentence of death taking effect (2:17; 3:19; Romans 5:14, Henry). The verse displays the dead Adam. The verse marks the years: 930. The verse displays the life: great. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the refrain: the seal of the curse. The total displays Adam's long life ending in death. The verse displays the days: numbered. The verse displays the Adam: died. The verse displays the sentence: fulfilled. The total of Adam's years and his death display the first toll of the chapter's refrain: all the days of Adam were nine hundred and thirty years, and he died — the great life of the first man ending in the grave, the sentence of death (3:19) sealed by the refrain "and he died." The verse displays the dead Adam, his line continuing in Seth (vv.6-8).
6:
"And Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos:" The brief verse notes Seth begetting Enos, continuing the line. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Henry note that "Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos" — Seth, the head of the godly line (4:25-26), begat Enos at 105 years; the line of promise continues from Adam through Seth to Enos; in Enos's day men began to call on the name of the LORD (4:26).

On "Seth lived an hundred and five years," the witnesses note the patriarch. Gill notes that Seth, the appointed seed, lived and begat in the line of promise; the godly line is carefully traced, generation by generation; the faithfulness of God in preserving the line through which the promised seed would come.

On "begat Enos," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the godly succession: "begat Enos" — Enos (whose name means "man," frail, mortal), the son of Seth, in whose time the public worship of God began (4:26); the line of Seth, marked by the worship of the LORD, in contrast to the line of Cain; the genealogy of the godly carries the promise forward; the continuance of the line of faith. The verse displays Seth begetting Enos, continuing the line.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos," Seth begetting Enos, the line of promise continuing (4:25-26, Gill, Henry); Seth the appointed seed in the godly line (Gill); "begat Enos," Enos in whose day men called on the LORD (4:26), the godly succession (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Seth. The verse marks the age: 105 years. The verse displays the son: Enos. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the worship: of the LORD. The succession displays the godly line carried from Seth to Enos. The verse displays the Seth: begetting. The verse displays the Enos: born. The verse displays the line: preserved. Seth begetting Enos, continuing the line, displays the godly succession: Seth lived a hundred and five years and begat Enos — the line of promise continuing from Adam through Seth to Enos, the godly line, marked by the worship of the LORD (4:26), carried forward toward the promised seed. The verse displays Seth's line, in his long life and death (vv.7-8).
7:
"And Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters:" The very brief verse notes Seth's long life and many children after Enos. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Gill and JFB note that "Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters" — Seth, like the other antediluvian patriarchs, lived to a great age (eight hundred and seven years after Enos) and begat many children; the long life and numerous offspring of the patriarchs.

On "eight hundred and seven years," the witnesses note the great age. Gill notes that Seth's long life, like Adam's (v.4), is part of the pattern of the antediluvians' great longevity; the patriarchs lived for centuries, seeing many generations; the long lives served to people the earth and preserve the line.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical and reformed JFB note the multiplied offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Seth had many other children besides Enos; the race multiplied through the numerous offspring of the patriarchs; the genealogy names the line of promise (Enos) but records the many sons and daughters; the increase of mankind through the long-lived fathers. The verse displays Seth's long life and many children after Enos.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the longevity — "Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters," Seth's great age and many children, the antediluvian pattern (Gill, JFB); the long life part of the patriarchs' great longevity (Gill); "begat sons and daughters," Seth's many other children, the race multiplying (grammatical-historical, JFB). The verse displays the long-lived Seth. The verse marks the years: 807. The verse displays the longevity: great. The verse displays the children: sons and daughters. The verse displays the increase: of the race. The long life displays Seth living and multiplying after Enos. The verse displays the years: many. The verse displays the offspring: numerous. The verse displays the line: continuing. Seth's long life and many children after Enos display the antediluvian pattern: Seth lived eight hundred and seven years after Enos and begat sons and daughters — the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race through his offspring. The verse displays the multiplying line, before Seth's death (v.8).
8:
"And all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: and he died." The very brief verse notes the total of Seth's years and his death. The grammatical voices note the sum and the refrain. Gill notes that "all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: and he died" — Seth's full life, nine hundred and twelve years, a great age like the other antediluvians; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the solemn toll that follows each patriarch; Seth, the godly head of the line of promise, died like the rest.

On "nine hundred and twelve years," the witnesses note the long life. Gill notes that Seth lived over nine centuries — among the longest-lived of the patriarchs; yet his long life, like all, came to its end; the great age could not avert death.

On "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — the recurring seal of mortality (vv.5, 11, 14, etc.); the sentence of death (3:19) takes effect on Seth as on Adam; even the godly patriarch of the line of promise dies; the refrain tolls through the chapter, marking that death reigns over all the sons of Adam (Romans 5:12, 14), save Enoch (v.24); the long lives end in the grave. The verse displays the total of Seth's years and his death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years," Seth's great age (Gill); the long life ending in death (Gill); "and he died," the refrain of mortality, the sentence of death taking effect (3:19; Romans 5:12, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Seth. The verse marks the years: 912. The verse displays the life: great. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the refrain: of mortality. The total displays Seth's long life ending in death. The verse displays the days: numbered. The verse displays the Seth: died. The verse displays the sentence: fulfilled. The verse displays the dead Seth, his line continuing in Enos (vv.9-11).
9:
"And Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan:" The very brief verse notes Enos begetting Cainan, continuing the line. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Ellicott note that "Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan" — Enos, the son of Seth (in whose day men called on the LORD, 4:26), begat Cainan at ninety years; the line of promise continues from Seth through Enos to Cainan; the godly genealogy is carried forward.

On "Enos lived ninety years," the witnesses note the patriarch. Gill notes that Enos, the third from Adam in the godly line, lived and begat in the line of faith; the genealogy traces the line carefully; God preserves the line of promise generation by generation.

On "begat Cainan," the grammatical-historical note the godly succession: "begat Cainan" — Cainan (a name distinct from Cain), the son of Enos, the next in the line of Seth; the line of the godly continues toward Noah; the careful record of the generations marks God's faithfulness in preserving the seed through which the promise would come; the line of faith carried forward. The verse displays Enos begetting Cainan, continuing the line.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the line of Enos. The verse marks the age: 90 years. The verse displays the son: Cainan. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the faithfulness: of God. The verse displays Enos's long life and many children after Cainan.

Gill notes that "all the days of Enos were nine hundred and five years: and he died" — Enos's full life, nine hundred and five years, a great age; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the recurring toll of mortality; Enos, the godly patriarch in whose day men called on the LORD (4:26), died like the rest.

12:
"And Cainan lived seventy years, and begat Mahalaleel:" The very brief verse notes Cainan begetting Mahalaleel, continuing the line. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill notes that "Cainan lived seventy years, and begat Mahalaleel" — Cainan, the son of Enos, begat Mahalaleel at seventy years; the line of promise continues from Enos through Cainan to Mahalaleel; the godly genealogy is carried forward.

On "Cainan lived seventy years," the witnesses note the patriarch. Gill notes that Cainan, the fourth from Adam in the godly line, lived and begat in the line of faith; the genealogy traces the line carefully, generation by generation; God preserves the line of promise.

On "begat Mahalaleel," the grammatical-historical note the godly succession: "begat Mahalaleel" — Mahalaleel (whose name means "praise of God" or "God is praised"), the son of Cainan, the next in the line of Seth; the line of the godly continues toward Noah; the name "Mahalaleel," praising God, fits the godly line that called on the LORD; the line of faith carried forward. The verse displays Cainan begetting Mahalaleel, continuing the line.

14:
"And all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: and he died." The very brief verse notes the total of Cainan's years and his death. The grammatical voices note the sum and the refrain. Gill notes that "all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: and he died" — Cainan's full life, nine hundred and ten years, a great age; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the recurring seal of mortality; Cainan, the godly patriarch of the line of promise, died like the rest.

The verse displays the total of Cainan's years and his death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years," Cainan's great age (Gill); the long life ending in death (Gill); "and he died," the refrain of mortality, the sentence of death (3:19; Romans 5:14, grammatical-historical). 15:
"And Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared:" The very brief verse notes Mahalaleel begetting Jared, continuing the line. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill notes that "Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared" — Mahalaleel, the son of Cainan, begat Jared at sixty-five years; the line of promise continues from Cainan through Mahalaleel to Jared; the godly genealogy is carried forward toward Noah.

On "Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years," the witnesses note the patriarch. Gill notes that Mahalaleel (whose name means "praise of God"), the fifth from Adam in the godly line, lived and begat in the line of faith; the genealogy traces the line carefully; God preserves the line of promise generation by generation.

On "begat Jared," the grammatical-historical note the godly succession: "begat Jared" — Jared (whose name may mean "descent"), the son of Mahalaleel, the next in the line of Seth; the line of the godly continues; in Jared's line would come Enoch, who walked with God (vv.18-24); the line of faith carried forward toward the great patriarchs. The verse displays Mahalaleel begetting Jared, continuing the line.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared," Mahalaleel begetting Jared, the line continuing (Gill); Mahalaleel in the godly line of faith (Gill); "begat Jared," the next in the line of Seth, the godly succession (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Mahalaleel. The verse marks the age: 65 years. The verse displays the son: Jared. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the descendant: Enoch to come. The succession displays the godly line carried from Mahalaleel to Jared. The verse displays the Mahalaleel: begetting. The verse displays the Jared: born. The verse displays the line: preserved. Mahalaleel begetting Jared, continuing the line, displays the godly succession: Mahalaleel lived sixty-five years and begat Jared — the line of promise continuing from Cainan through Mahalaleel to Jared, in whose line would come Enoch, the godly genealogy carried forward. The verse displays the line of Mahalaleel, in his long life and death (vv.16-17).
16:
"And Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters:" The very brief verse notes Mahalaleel's long life and many children after Jared. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Gill notes that "Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters" — Mahalaleel, like the other patriarchs, lived to a great age (eight hundred and thirty years after Jared) and begat many children; the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers.

On "eight hundred and thirty years," the witnesses note the great age. Gill notes that Mahalaleel's long life is part of the antediluvian pattern; the patriarchs lived for centuries; the long lives served God's purpose to people the earth and preserve the line of promise through few generations.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Mahalaleel had many other children besides Jared; the race multiplied through the patriarchs' offspring; the genealogy names the line of promise but records the many sons and daughters; the increase of mankind. The verse displays Mahalaleel's long life and many children after Jared.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the long-lived Mahalaleel. The verse marks the years: 830. The verse displays the longevity: great. The verse displays the children: sons and daughters. 17:
"And all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and five years: and he died." The very brief verse notes the total of Mahalaleel's years and his death. The grammatical voices note the sum and the refrain. Gill notes that "all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and five years: and he died" — Mahalaleel's full life, eight hundred ninety-five years, a great age; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the recurring toll of mortality; Mahalaleel, the godly patriarch whose name praises God, died like the rest.

On "eight hundred ninety and five years," the witnesses note the long life. Gill notes that Mahalaleel lived nearly nine centuries; yet his long life, like all, came to its end; the great age could not exempt him from death.

On "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — the seal of mortality repeated over each name (vv.5, 8, 11, 14); the sentence of death (3:19) takes effect; even the godly patriarch who praised God dies; death reigns over all the sons of Adam (Romans 5:12, 14), save Enoch (v.24); the long lives end in the grave; the refrain marks the universal reign of death from the fall. The verse displays the total of Mahalaleel's years and his death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 18:
"And Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch:" The brief verse notes Jared begetting Enoch, the godly patriarch who walked with God. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Ellicott note that "Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch" — Jared, the son of Mahalaleel, begat Enoch at one hundred sixty-two years; the line of promise continues from Mahalaleel through Jared to Enoch; and Enoch is the notable patriarch who walked with God and was translated (vv.21-24).

On "Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years," the witnesses note the patriarch. Gill and Ellicott note that Jared, the sixth from Adam in the godly line, lived and begat in the line of faith; the genealogy traces the line carefully; Jared's significance lies chiefly in his son Enoch, the seventh from Adam (Jude 14), the most eminent of the antediluvian patriarchs.

On "he begat Enoch," the grammatical-historical note the godly Enoch: "he begat Enoch" — Enoch (a different Enoch from Cain's son, 4:17), the son of Jared, the seventh from Adam; this Enoch is the patriarch who walked with God and was taken without dying (vv.22-24); the genealogy, otherwise a record of death, comes to the one man who did not die; Jared's son Enoch is the bright exception in the chapter. The verse displays Jared begetting Enoch.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch," Jared begetting Enoch, the line continuing to the notable Enoch (Gill, Ellicott); Jared in the godly line of faith, his significance in his son (Gill, Ellicott); "he begat Enoch," the godly Enoch, the seventh from Adam, who walked with God (Jude 14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Jared. The verse marks the age: 162 years. The verse displays the son: Enoch. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the begotten Enoch, in Jared's long life and death (vv.19-20).
19:
"And Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and begat sons and daughters:" The very brief verse notes Jared's long life and many children after Enoch. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Gill and JFB note that "Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and begat sons and daughters" — Jared, like the other patriarchs, lived to a great age (eight hundred years after Enoch) and begat many children; the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers.

On "eight hundred years," the witnesses note the great age. Gill notes that Jared's long life is part of the antediluvian pattern; the patriarchs lived for centuries; the long lives served to people the earth and preserve the line; Jared lived to see many generations of his descendants.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical and reformed JFB note the offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Jared had many other children besides Enoch; the race multiplied through the patriarchs' offspring; the genealogy names the line of promise but records the many sons and daughters; the increase of mankind. The verse displays Jared's long life and many children after Enoch.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. 20:
"And all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died." The very brief verse notes the total of Jared's years and his death. The grammatical voices note the sum and the refrain. Gill notes that "all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died" — Jared's full life, nine hundred sixty-two years, among the longest of the patriarchs; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the recurring toll of mortality; Jared, the father of the godly Enoch, died like the rest.

On "nine hundred sixty and two years," the witnesses note the long life. Gill notes that Jared lived over nine and a half centuries, one of the longest-lived of the antediluvians (second to Methuselah); yet his long life, like all, ended in death; the great age could not avert the sentence.

On "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — the seal of mortality repeated over each name; the sentence of death (3:19) takes effect; even Jared, the father of Enoch (who did not die), dies himself; the contrast is striking — Jared dies, but his son Enoch is taken without death (v.24); death reigns over all the sons of Adam (Romans 5:14), save the one exception; the long lives end in the grave. The verse displays the total of Jared's years and his death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years," Jared's great age, among the longest (Gill); the long life ending in death (Gill); "and he died," the refrain of mortality, contrasting his son Enoch who did not die (v.24, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Jared. The verse marks the years: 962. The verse displays the life: great. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the contrast: with Enoch. 21:
"And Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah:" The brief verse notes Enoch begetting Methuselah, the longest-lived patriarch. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Henry note that "Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah" — Enoch, the seventh from Adam (Jude 14), begat Methuselah at sixty-five years; the line of promise continues from Jared through Enoch to Methuselah; Methuselah would become the longest-lived of all men (v.27).

On "Enoch lived sixty and five years," the puritan Henry notes the patriarch. Henry notes that Enoch, the godly patriarch who walked with God (vv.22, 24), began (as the text will show) to walk with God especially after the birth of Methuselah; the begetting of his son marked a turning in his godly walk; Enoch is the most eminent of the antediluvians.

On "begat Methuselah," the grammatical-historical note the named son: "begat Methuselah" — Methuselah (whose name is variously interpreted, some reading it "when he dies, it shall be sent," a possible reference to the flood, which came the year of Methuselah's death); the longest-lived man (969 years, v.27); the line of promise continues through Methuselah to Lamech and Noah; God's patience is marked in the long life of Methuselah before the flood. The verse displays Enoch begetting Methuselah.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah," Enoch begetting Methuselah, the line continuing (Gill, Henry); Enoch the godly patriarch, his walk with God marked after Methuselah's birth (Henry); "begat Methuselah," the longest-lived man, in whose death-year the flood came (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Enoch. The verse marks the age: 65 years. The verse displays the son: Methuselah. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the begotten Methuselah, while Enoch walks with God (v.22).
22:
"And Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years, and begat sons and daughters:" The witnesses note Enoch walking with God for three hundred years. On "Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years," the grammatical voices note the godly walk. Gill and Clarke note that "Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years" — instead of the usual formula ("lived... years"), the text says Enoch "walked with God"; for three hundred years after Methuselah's birth, Enoch walked with God; the phrase marks a life of intimate fellowship with God, holiness, and obedience; Enoch's whole life was a walk with God.

On "walked with God," the reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the fellowship. Calvin and Wesley note that to "walk with God" is to live in close communion and agreement with him (Amos 3:3, "can two walk together, except they be agreed?"); a life of faith, holiness, and continual fellowship; Enoch pleased God (Hebrews 11:5), believing and obeying him; his walk with God was the pattern of the godly life amid an ungodly generation; Enoch also prophesied (Jude 14-15).

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the family: "begat sons and daughters" — Enoch, even in his close walk with God, lived an ordinary family life, begetting children; the godly walk is compatible with the duties and relations of life; holiness is lived out in the family and the world. The verse displays Enoch walking with God for three hundred years.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the walk — "Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years," Enoch's life of intimate fellowship with God, the special formula marking his godliness (Gill, Clarke); "walked with God," the close communion, faith, and holiness of Enoch (Amos 3:3; Hebrews 11:5, Calvin, Wesley); "begat sons and daughters," the godly walk lived out in family life (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the walking Enoch. The verse marks the walk: with God. The verse displays the years: three hundred. The verse displays the fellowship: intimate. The verse displays the family: sons and daughters. The walk displays Enoch in continual fellowship with God. The verse displays the Enoch: walking. The verse displays the God: with him. The verse displays the holiness: lived. Enoch walking with God for three hundred years displays the godly life amid an ungodly age: Enoch walked with God three hundred years and begat sons and daughters — a life of intimate fellowship, faith, and holiness, pleasing God, lived out even in the relations of family. The verse displays the walking Enoch, whose days are numbered before his translation (v.23).
23:
"And all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years:" The very brief verse notes the total of Enoch's years — and the absence of the usual death-refrain. The grammatical voices note the shorter span. Gill notes that "all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years" — Enoch's life was three hundred sixty-five years; notably shorter than the other antediluvians (who lived eight or nine centuries); Enoch's days were comparatively few, for he was taken early (v.24).

On "three hundred sixty and five years," the witnesses note the contrast. Gill notes that Enoch's three hundred sixty-five years, while a full life by later reckoning, was short among the long-lived antediluvians; yet his life was not cut short by death but by translation (v.24); God took him in the midst of his years, not as a punishment but as a reward.

On the absence of "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the striking omission: unlike every other patriarch in the chapter (vv.5, 8, 11, etc.), the refrain "and he died" is not said of Enoch; the omission prepares for v.24, "and he was not; for God took him" — Enoch did not die, but was translated; the breaking of the chapter's death-refrain marks Enoch as the great exception; the man who walked with God did not see death. The verse displays the total of Enoch's years and the absence of the death-refrain.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the span — "all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years," Enoch's comparatively short life among the antediluvians (Gill); his days few, for he was taken early (v.24, Gill); the striking absence of "and he died," preparing for his translation (v.24, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the days of Enoch. The verse marks the years: 365. The verse displays the span: short. The verse displays the omission: no death-refrain. The verse displays the exception: Enoch. The total displays Enoch's days ending not in death but in translation. The verse displays the days: numbered. The verse displays the death: not said. The verse displays the Enoch: excepted. The total of Enoch's years and the absence of the death-refrain display the great exception of the chapter: all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty-five years — comparatively few, and notably without the refrain "and he died," preparing for the wonder that Enoch did not die but was taken by God. The verse displays the days of Enoch, ended by his translation (v.24).
24:
"And Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him." The witnesses note Enoch's walk with God and his translation — the crux of "God took him." On "Enoch walked with God," the grammatical voices note the godly life. Ellicott and Gill note that "Enoch walked with God" (repeated from v.22) — the summary of Enoch's whole life: a walk of faith, holiness, and intimate fellowship with God; the LXX renders it "Enoch pleased God" (the basis of Hebrews 11:5, "by faith Enoch was translated... he pleased God"); Enoch's godliness is set as the reason for his translation.

On "and he was not; for God took him," interpreters expound the translation. The grammatical-historical Ellicott, the reformed Calvin, and the wesleyan-arminian Wesley hold that Enoch was translated — taken bodily to heaven without dying, as Elijah later was (2 Kings 2:11); "he was not (found among men); for God took him (to himself)" — God removed Enoch from the earth without death; Hebrews 11:5 plainly teaches this: "Enoch was translated that he should not see death." The translation is a reward of his faith and a pledge of immortality; the man who walked with God did not die but was taken to God. A minority/rationalist reading takes "God took him" as a euphemism for an early death (Ellicott notes this reading and rejects it, citing Hebrews 11:5).

On "for God took him," the reformed Calvin notes the pledge: Enoch's translation, in an age of death and impending judgment, is a testimony to the immortality of the soul and the hope of life beyond death; God's taking of Enoch shows that the godly have a hope beyond the grave; the bright exception in the genealogy of death. The verse displays Enoch's walk with God and his translation.

The witnesses agree the substance: Enoch walked with God and was taken by God — not dying, but translated to heaven (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Hebrews 11:5). The phrase "God took him" bears a crux: Enoch translated bodily to heaven without dying, like Elijah (the dominant reading, Hebrews 11:5) vs a euphemism for an early death (the minority/rationalist reading, rejected by Ellicott). The verse displays the translation crux. The verse marks the walk: with God. The verse displays the debate: translation or early death. The verse displays the taking: by God. The verse displays the reward: of faith. The translation displays Enoch taken to God without death. The verse displays the Enoch: walking. The verse displays the God: took him. The verse displays the death: not seen. Enoch's walk with God and his translation display the great wonder of the chapter and a crux: Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him — read by the dominant view as his translation to heaven without death (Hebrews 11:5; like Elijah), and by a minority as a euphemism for early death; the man who walked with God did not see death but was taken to him. The verse displays the translated Enoch, while his son Methuselah lives on (vv.25-27).
25:
"And Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech:" The brief verse notes Methuselah begetting Lamech, continuing the line. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Henry note that "Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech" — Methuselah, the son of the translated Enoch, begat Lamech at one hundred eighty-seven years; the line of promise continues from Enoch through Methuselah to Lamech (the father of Noah); the godly genealogy carries on toward the flood and Noah.

On "Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years," the witnesses note the long-lived patriarch. Gill and Henry note that Methuselah, the longest-lived of all men (969 years, v.27), is the son of Enoch and the grandfather of Noah; his very long life spans much of the antediluvian age; God's patience is shown in the long life of Methuselah before the flood (which came in the year of his death).

On "begat Lamech," the grammatical-historical note the godly succession: "begat Lamech" — Lamech (a different Lamech from Cain's line, 4:18), the son of Methuselah, the father of Noah; the line of promise continues; the godly Lamech (in contrast to the boastful Lamech of Cain's line) names his son Noah in hope of comfort (v.29); the line of faith carried to Noah. The verse displays Methuselah begetting Lamech.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech," Methuselah begetting Lamech, the line continuing to Noah's father (Gill, Henry); Methuselah the longest-lived, son of Enoch, grandfather of Noah (Gill, Henry); "begat Lamech," the godly Lamech, father of Noah (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Methuselah. The verse marks the age: 187 years. The verse displays the son: Lamech. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the patriarch: longest-lived. The succession displays the godly line carried from Methuselah to Lamech. The verse displays the Methuselah: begetting. The verse displays the Lamech: born. The verse displays the line: preserved. Methuselah begetting Lamech, continuing the line, displays the godly succession: Methuselah lived a hundred eighty-seven years and begat Lamech — the line of promise continuing from Enoch through Methuselah, the longest-lived of men, to Lamech, the father of Noah. The verse displays the line of Methuselah, in his unequaled long life and death (vv.26-27).
26:
"And Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters:" The very brief verse notes Methuselah's long life and many children after Lamech. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Gill notes that "Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters" — Methuselah, the longest-lived of men, lived seven hundred eighty-two years after Lamech and begat many children; his very long life (totaling 969 years, v.27) is the greatest of all the antediluvians.

On "seven hundred eighty and two years," the witnesses note the unequaled age. Gill notes that Methuselah's life, the longest in Scripture, spans the whole age from near Adam's time to the flood; God's patience and forbearance are marked in the long life of Methuselah, whose death (in his 969th year) coincided with the coming of the flood; the long life is a testimony to God's longsuffering before judgment.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Methuselah had many other children besides Lamech; the race multiplied through the patriarchs' offspring; the genealogy names the line of promise but records the many sons and daughters; the increase of mankind before the flood. The verse displays Methuselah's long life and many children after Lamech.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the longevity — "Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters," Methuselah's unequaled long life and many children (Gill); the longest life in Scripture, marking God's patience before the flood (Gill); "begat sons and daughters," the race multiplying through his offspring (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the long-lived Methuselah. The verse marks the years: 782. The verse displays the longevity: unequaled. The verse displays the children: sons and daughters. The verse displays the patience: God's. The long life displays Methuselah living and multiplying, the longest-lived of men. The verse displays the years: many. The verse displays the offspring: numerous. The verse displays the line: continuing. Methuselah's long life and many children after Lamech display the unequaled age of the patriarch: Methuselah lived seven hundred eighty-two years after Lamech and begat sons and daughters — the longest life of all men, marking God's patience and forbearance before the flood. The verse displays the long-lived Methuselah, before his death in the flood-year (v.27).
27:
"And all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years: and he died." The brief verse notes the total of Methuselah's years — the longest life — and his death. The grammatical voices note the unequaled sum. Gill and Clarke note that "all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years: and he died" — Methuselah lived nine hundred sixty-nine years, the longest life recorded in Scripture; and then the refrain, "and he died" — the longest-lived of all men died at last; even the greatest age ends in death.

On "nine hundred sixty and nine years," the witnesses note the longest life. Clarke and Gill note that Methuselah's nine hundred sixty-nine years is the greatest age of any man; his long life is a monument to God's patience; notably, by the chronology, Methuselah died in the very year of the flood (his name, on one reading, "when he dies, it shall be sent," pointing to the flood); God's longsuffering waited through Methuselah's whole long life before sending the judgment.

On "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — even Methuselah, the longest-lived, dies; the refrain seals the chapter's theme — death reigns over all the sons of Adam (Romans 5:14), and the longest life is no escape; the great age only magnifies the certainty of death; the longest-lived patriarch, like all, ends in the grave (save Enoch, v.24). The verse displays the total of Methuselah's years and his death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unequaled sum — "all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years," the longest life recorded (Gill, Clarke); the great age a monument to God's patience, Methuselah dying in the flood-year (Clarke, Gill); "and he died," the refrain, even the longest-lived dying (Romans 5:14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Methuselah. The verse marks the years: 969. The verse displays the life: longest. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the patience: God's. The total displays the longest life ending in death. The verse displays the days: most. The verse displays the Methuselah: died. The verse displays the flood: at hand. The total of Methuselah's years and his death display the longest life ending in the grave: all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty-nine years, and he died — the longest life of any man, a monument to God's patience, ending at last in death in the very year of the flood. The verse displays the dead Methuselah, the line continuing in Lamech and Noah (vv.28-29).
28:
"And Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son:" The brief verse notes Lamech begetting a son, the future Noah. The grammatical voices note the succession. Gill and Henry note that "Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son" — Lamech, the son of Methuselah, begat a son (Noah, named in v.29) at one hundred eighty-two years; the line of promise continues from Methuselah through Lamech to Noah; the godly genealogy reaches its goal in Noah, through whom the race and the line would be preserved through the flood.

On "Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years," the puritan Henry notes the godly Lamech. Henry notes that Lamech, of the godly line of Seth (a different Lamech from Cain's boastful descendant, 4:18-24), lived and begat in the line of faith; the genealogy carries the line to the threshold of Noah; the godly Lamech's hope (v.29) contrasts with the violent boast of Cain's Lamech.

On "begat a son," the grammatical-historical note the future Noah: "begat a son" — the son, not yet named in this verse, is Noah (v.29), the tenth from Adam; through Noah the line of promise would survive the flood; Noah is the bridge from the antediluvian world to the new world after the flood; the line of faith reaches its preserver in Noah. The verse displays Lamech begetting a son, the future Noah.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the succession — "Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son," Lamech begetting Noah, the line continuing (Gill, Henry); the godly Lamech in the line of faith (Henry); "begat a son," the future Noah, the tenth from Adam, the preserver of the line (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the line of Lamech. The verse marks the age: 182 years. The verse displays the son: Noah. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the preserver: Noah to come. The succession displays the godly line carried from Lamech to Noah. The verse displays the Lamech: begetting. The verse displays the son: born. The verse displays the line: preserved. Lamech begetting a son, the future Noah, displays the line continuing to its preserver: Lamech lived a hundred eighty-two years and begat a son — Noah, the tenth from Adam, through whom the line of promise would survive the flood. The verse displays the begotten Noah, named in hope (v.29).
29:
"And he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed." The witnesses note Lamech naming Noah in hope of comfort — the crux of the hope. On "he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort us," the grammatical voices note the naming and hope. Ellicott and Gill note that "he called his name Noah (rest/comfort), saying, This same shall comfort us" — Lamech names his son Noah (whose name relates to "rest" or "comfort," from nuach/nacham), declaring a hope that this son would bring comfort; the name expresses Lamech's longing for relief.

On "concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed," interpreters expound the comfort hoped for. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed Calvin note the relief from toil: Lamech hopes for comfort "concerning our work and toil... because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed" — relief from the wearisome toil of cultivating the cursed ground (3:17-19); the hope that Noah would bring some easing of the curse and labor; fulfilled, on one reading, in Noah's agricultural discoveries (the cultivation of the vine, 9:20), or in the renewal of the earth after the flood (when the curse was, in a measure, eased, 8:21-22). A second reading takes Lamech's hope as Messianic-tinged: a longing for the promised seed (3:15) who would bring true rest and comfort from the curse; whether Lamech thought Noah might be the deliverer, or hoped in the promise through him; the deeper hope of redemption breathes through the name.

On "the ground which the LORD hath cursed," the grammatical-historical note the burden: Lamech's words look back to the curse on the ground (3:17); the toil under the curse weighed on the godly; the hope of comfort and rest expresses the longing of fallen man for relief and, ultimately, for the Redeemer. The verse displays Lamech naming Noah in hope of comfort.

The witnesses agree the substance: Lamech named his son Noah ("comfort/rest"), hoping he would bring comfort concerning the toil of the cursed ground (Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The hope bears a crux: relief from the toil of the cursed ground — through Noah's agricultural discovery or the post-flood renewal of the earth (the literal reading — Clarke, Calvin) vs a Messianic-tinged hope, a longing for the promised seed and true rest from the curse (the deeper reading). The verse displays the comfort crux. The verse marks the name: Noah, comfort. The verse displays the hope: of comfort. The verse displays the debate: relief from toil, or Messianic hope. The verse displays the curse: on the ground. The naming displays Lamech's hope of rest through Noah. The verse displays the Noah: named. The verse displays the comfort: hoped. The verse displays the toil: eased. Lamech naming Noah in hope of comfort displays the longing of the godly and a crux: Lamech named his son Noah, hoping he would comfort them concerning their toil because of the cursed ground — read as the hope of relief from labor through Noah (his agriculture, or the post-flood renewal) and, more deeply, as a Messianic-tinged longing for true rest from the curse through the promised seed. The verse displays the named Noah, in Lamech's long life and death (vv.30-31).
30:
"And Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters:" The very brief verse notes Lamech's long life and many children after Noah. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Gill notes that "Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters" — Lamech, like the other patriarchs, lived to a great age (five hundred ninety-five years after Noah) and begat many children; the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers (though Lamech's total, 777 years, is somewhat shorter than most).

On "five hundred ninety and five years," the witnesses note the age. Gill notes that Lamech's long life is part of the antediluvian pattern, though his total (v.31) is less than the others; the patriarchs lived for centuries; the long lives served to people the earth and preserve the line of promise to Noah.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Lamech had many other children besides Noah; the race multiplied through the patriarchs' offspring; the genealogy names the line of promise (Noah) but records the many sons and daughters; the increase of mankind before the flood. The verse displays Lamech's long life and many children after Noah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the longevity — "Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters," Lamech's great age and many children (Gill); the long life part of the antediluvian pattern, though shorter (Gill); "begat sons and daughters," the race multiplying through his offspring (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the long-lived Lamech. The verse marks the years: 595. The verse displays the longevity: great. The verse displays the children: sons and daughters. The verse displays the increase: of the race. The long life displays Lamech living and multiplying after Noah. The verse displays the years: many. The verse displays the offspring: numerous. The verse displays the line: continuing. Lamech's long life and many children after Noah display the antediluvian pattern: Lamech lived five hundred ninety-five years after Noah and begat sons and daughters — the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race through his offspring. The verse displays the multiplying line, before Lamech's death (v.31).
31:
"And all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years: and he died." The very brief verse notes the total of Lamech's years and his death. The grammatical voices note the sum and the refrain. Gill notes that "all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years: and he died" — Lamech's full life, seven hundred seventy-seven years (somewhat shorter than most of the patriarchs); and then the refrain, "and he died" — the recurring toll of mortality; Lamech, the godly father of Noah, died like the rest, before the flood.

On "seven hundred seventy and seven years," the witnesses note the life. Gill notes that Lamech lived seven hundred seventy-seven years; his life, though long, was shorter than his father Methuselah's and others'; yet, like all, it ended in death; Lamech died before the flood (which came after his death, in the year of Methuselah's death).

On "and he died," the grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — the seal of mortality repeated over the last of the named patriarchs before Noah; the sentence of death (3:19) takes effect; even the godly Lamech, who hoped for comfort through Noah (v.29), dies; the refrain closes the line of the patriarchs in death; death reigns over all the sons of Adam (Romans 5:14), save Enoch; the long lives end in the grave, the chapter sealing the reign of death until the flood. The verse displays the total of Lamech's years and his death.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years," Lamech's life, somewhat shorter than most (Gill); the long life ending in death, before the flood (Gill); "and he died," the refrain of mortality, the sentence of death (3:19; Romans 5:14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Lamech. The verse marks the years: 777. The verse displays the life: long. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the refrain: of mortality. The total displays Lamech's life ending in death before the flood. The verse displays the days: numbered. The verse displays the Lamech: died. The verse displays the sentence: fulfilled. The total of Lamech's years and his death display the refrain over the father of Noah: all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy-seven years, and he died — the godly Lamech, who hoped for comfort through Noah, dying like the rest before the flood, the sentence of death sealed by the refrain. The verse displays the dead Lamech, the line resting in Noah (v.32).
32:
"And Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth." The witnesses note Noah and the birth of his three sons. On "Noah was five hundred years old," the grammatical voices note the patriarch. Ellicott and Gill note that "Noah was five hundred years old" — Noah, the tenth from Adam, the preserver of the line through the flood; he begat his sons at an advanced age (around five hundred years and after); Noah is the goal of the genealogy, the bridge from the old world to the new; through him the race and the line of promise would survive the flood.

On "Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the three sons. Calvin and Gill note that "Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth" — Noah's three sons, from whom the whole post-flood race would descend (9:18-19; 10:1); the three sons are named together, though born over a period; through these three, the earth would be repeopled after the flood; the line of promise continues especially through Shem (in whose line would come Abraham and Christ).

On the three sons, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the new beginning: Shem, Ham, and Japheth are the heads of the post-flood nations (ch. 10); the whole human race after the flood springs from them; the genealogy of ch. 5, having traced the line from Adam to Noah, ends at the threshold of the flood and the new world; the line of promise, preserved through Noah, will continue through Shem to Abraham and to Christ. The verse displays Noah and the birth of his three sons.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the patriarch — "Noah was five hundred years old," Noah, the tenth from Adam, the preserver of the line (Ellicott, Gill); "Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth," Noah's three sons, from whom the post-flood race descended (9:18-19; 10:1, Calvin, Gill); the three the heads of the new world's nations, the line continuing through Shem (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Noah's sons. The verse marks the age: five hundred. The verse displays the patriarch: Noah. The verse displays the sons: Shem, Ham, Japheth. The verse displays the new beginning: through them. The genealogy displays Noah and the heads of the post-flood race. The verse displays the Noah: aged. The verse displays the sons: three. The verse displays the line: preserved. Noah and the birth of his three sons display the goal of the genealogy: Noah was five hundred years old and begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth — Noah, the tenth from Adam, the preserver of the line, and his three sons, from whom the whole post-flood race would spring, the line of promise continuing through Shem. The verse closes the genealogy of Adam to Noah, at the threshold of the flood (ch.6ff.).
